speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number five of the interview with Eva Neumann, June 4, 1996. You were telling me about being in Neustadt--

speaker: interviewee
text: Neustadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Neustadt--

speaker: interviewee
text: Glewe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Glewe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So anybody who died in this room, so called, they put on a pile, whoever couldn't move. But I think most of us were-- another day, all of us would have been there. And it seems, which I don't remember which I was passed conscious, I was on this pile it seems. I was told. And a Russian doctor who happened to have been Jewish got hold of my arm. Not my arm, he was trying to tidy up and was pulling the bodies. And he realized that I was still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then he took pity on me, whatever. And he took me into a hospital. And it seems over there, for quite a long time, unconscious. And he started feeding me, which was very good. Because those girls who looted and fed themselves, they died there. So I was very lucky, because he started feeding me slowly. And he kind of took-- brought me around and made me as well as-- well, you know. And he took me in. And he gave me some advice.

speaker: interviewee
text: He talked to me. And he said, you know, you have been evacuated, freed by the Russians. And you've got to watch them. They are not going to treat you like poor little girls who just survived the camp. They're very rough. They've been in the war too many years. They do things tot he girls you wouldn't want to. I'll give you a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] things around your arm which says typhoid. And they're very frightened of infectious diseases.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he gave me this thing. And whoever was with me, these certain girls would survived this camp, we kept together. And this protected us from the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we started walking in Germany trying to get hitchhiking to the next town, the next town. Some lorries used to pick us up. Some horse and carts used to pick us up, used to go into German houses. And they kind of fed us. In the beginning, we looted a little bit. But it was too-- it wasn't nice. We couldn't do it. But we just said, we are refugees. And most of them gave us to eat. And we start trying to get back, back to, well, home, so called. One day I was in a town, no idea what town it was. And I noticed in the middle of the square, the middle of this German town, four brand new German buses. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I still spoke Czech at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went up to the driver. I said, who are you? What are you? So he said they're collecting Czech refugees to take them back to Prague. So I said, I am Czech. And I said, OK. And who are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they couldn't speak Czech, one or two maybe. I say, we are all one transport. So they said, all right, hop into the bus. And they took us, gave us to eat, and dealt with us like human beings. And they took us to Prague. In Prague, we were-- we had to go to the Jewish joint the-- was kind of like collecting all the refugees. And there, we got some mattresses and food. And a lot of people collected there waiting for relations. So-- so we hung around for about three or four weeks in Prague hoping meeting somebody or-- you work in the crematorium, you know. And you know your parents have gone in there, and you [INAUDIBLE]. But when reality sets in, you don't believe it. And that's when the real hardship and pain comes in, when, you know, reality is-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when you-- when you face reality. Anyhow, we stayed in Prague, didn't meet anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that there are-- that there are feelings that you-- that you-- that you sort of had to hold together while you were going through, and then once it was over, there was a of some kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't feelings. It was the non-belief, the belief that- that can't-- can't happen. You know, like, somebody must be alive. It's like it's a dream. And if they did go into the crematorium, I didn't see that body. They could have gone out on the other side. You know, human nature is a very funny thing. You kind yourself. You pretend. You don't want to believe what you-- what you can't take. Because if I would have realized at my age what really has happened, not pretending that it didn't happen, I probably wouldn't have survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because there was no-- why should I come home? There was nobody home. There was no home, nobody alive. So after Prague, I thought this is-- I've got to carry on. I've got to go home, not realizing then that there was no home. I couldn't go back to where I came. So, again, hitchhiking and wagons and where ever I could get a left. By that time, I think I was on my own. And we went back-- I went back to Budapest. In Budapest, I couldn't anymore. I started to swell up. I think eating and not eating, I reacted.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was, again, one of these Jewish centers where you could register. And you could look up the names to see if anybody has come back. When I came in, one of my cousins noticed me, recognized me. I didn't recognize her. After a time, I did. She escaped one of these eight sisters with her sister with pretending that they weren't Jewish, and then stayed in Budapest, this cousin, her sister, her husband, and two children. And they lived under false papers, changed their names, changed their appearance. And they managed to survive. And there, she noticed me. And she took me home to her sister, to this family. I don't know why they took me in, because thinking what I looked like and what I was. And I was still infected with lice and still had terrible, terrible typhoid, diarrhea, all sorts of illnesses. And they had two little children. But they did take me. And they were wonderful. And I stayed there for three, four years. And then I couldn't go home. I never went home again. Because they said, it's too dangerous. They won't let you back. They didn't-- some of mine, not relations, but friends, went back, couldn't come out again. The Russians-- it was then Russia by then. And they wouldn't let you out. So I stayed in Hungary for about three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I looked after these two little children, which I kind of feel they're mine. Because the adults went-- they were already working people I couldn't work. I was still-- took me a long, long, long time to get back to reasonable health. And then I started to study a bit, had a lot to catch up. I studied a bit. And then I got a job. And then about 1949, the Russians started to pick on me that I should join the Communist party. And if I don't join the communist party, I can't have a job, you know. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] so I decided I got to go. So at the time, everybody was going, going to Palestine at the time. So, again, started going from Budapest to another town, another town, with false paper, being caught at the border and put into prison, you know, like a nightmare, play it back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you put into prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I had no official, no passport, no nothing. I wasn't a nationality. I was running away from the communists. I-- I was-- I was nobody. I had no right to go. And that was the border between Austria. And they wouldn't-- so somehow, I managed to run away from there at night and walk to the Austrian border. A few of us were there in this prison. And at the border, again, the Austrians sent transport. And they took us into Vienna. And in Vienna, I was also-- again, there was a [INAUDIBLE] hospital that was transferred into this new lot of refugees. And I stayed there for a few months. And I was going to Marseilles to get onto the boat to go to Israel. And then everybody started panicking. You can't go to Palestine, because you're going to Cyprus. And if you want to go to Cyprus, you're back in prison again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once you knew, you didn't want to do it. And I had a wonderful cousin that lived in Geneva. He was a representative of the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Israeli [NON-ENGLISH]. He did the children's transport to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was working in the embassy there. And he said, you come to Geneva first. And when things quiet down, you can go to Israel. And I did that. I went to Geneva and stayed there for a year. Then I met my husband. And I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in Switzerland on holiday. And somebody introduced us. And he was-- he also comes from Czechoslovakia. But they managed to-- his father and brother and his sister managed to get out of there to England. And the rest of his family was taken to Auschwitz, four brothers and sisters, mother, and nieces and nephews. They all went to Auschwitz. They didn't know which way to run. One run [INAUDIBLE] to Hungary. And one run [INAUDIBLE] to England. Nobody knew which is the safest. And the one who run to Budapest, things got bad there. So he ran back to-- to Bratislava, to [INAUDIBLE]. And they were taken, all killed. And this, his father, my father-in-law, my brother-in-law, sister-in-law, and my husband managed to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leopold Neumann. And we got married and went back to England. I couldn't get out of Switzerland. I had trouble in Switzerland, because I wasn't allowed to be there. I had no passport. And so somehow I managed for nine months to one year-- I think a year-- to kind of get registered into a boarding school pretending I was a student. And it was a Jewish boarding school. And they backed me up. So they gave me-- every month, I had to renew my passport. This is called [NON-ENGLISH]. This is a passport they gave any non-nationality. And I stayed there for a year. They were wonderful to me, fantastic people. And then I got married and came to live in England. And I had five--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which did you do first, marry or come to England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marry. I couldn't come to England. I couldn't get out of the country. I couldn't stay. I couldn't get out. I couldn't do anything, because I was nothing. I had no-- so he came to Geneva. And we got married in the registrar. So I could come out and come to England. But-- and automatically, I became a British citizen. And then the Jewish wedding, the chuppah, and the everything was already in England. I [? couldn't have ?] stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your settle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you settle in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: I settled in Manchester. My husband lived in London till the bombing started. Because they started bombing London. And then he went to Manchester. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they settled and continued their original business, which was still Czechoslovakia. They had connections in-- in England. And they knew them and trusted them. And so they started the same business in Manchester. And they become [INAUDIBLE] merchants in Manchester.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you had a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had five children in Manchester, two sons and three daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yehoshua, Miriam, Samuel, Devorah, and Leah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I've got 19 grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: I won't make you name them all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: I won't make you name them all.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [LAUGHTER] I don't know if I could. And they live-- two live in London, two live in Manchester. And the younger one lives in between New York and London and Manchester and Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What has been important in your life since coming to England and settling down?

speaker: interviewee
text: The family, mainly the family. That's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's the only important thing. Nothing else mattered. That was the important thing and bringing them up in the nice Jewish tradition. And that was my aim, and I hope I achieved it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is the Jewish tradition something that's still very important to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Pardon.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you value in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. There are so many aspects of it. First of all, I was brought up. I never questioned it. That was my upbringing. Second of all, I survived. Maybe-- maybe there's something in it. And third of all, I don't know any other way. Maybe if I would have gone to Cyprus or maybe if I would have gone to the wrong place at the wrong time, but I didn't. I got to the right-- to now, looking back on it-- to the right place. I got to this cousin in Geneva, who was very religious. My husband was very religious. It didn't-- I never questioned to come another way. This was it. I was used to it. It was my life. That's how I wanted it. And that's how I feel secure. I'm very pleased now about it. Because my security for the children is partly the religion they come. It's a whole-- it's a unity. It's mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is your experience of the war something that you've spoken about?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never. Never. But lately, I-- the last couple of years, the Jewish Museum in Manchester asked me to make a tape. Because they're denying it. And we should remember it. And I made my first statement at the time, about three or four years ago. I never showed it to anybody till just very recently. And this is the second time I spoke about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it like to speak about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Difficult. Difficult, because you can't express what you feel. You know, whenever I see people speak about it and I'm with somebody who's been with me in Auschwitz, we look at each other and we think, what are they talking about? I think with anything in life, unless you experienced it, you can't 100% believe it. But that feeling of hunger and the feeling of despair or dehumanization of becoming an animal or being treated like something which is not human, you can't explain that to anybody. It's no way anybody who hasn't experienced it can understand what that means. I think maybe there are some people who understand it. But I find with even good things in life, unless you experience it or you laughed about it at the time, we can explain, oh, this is something we laugh about. That's what I think. I think this is too deep and too horrible and too tremendous to put it over. You have to feel it. You have to-- that type of hunger-- I mean, I fast on Yom Kippur [INAUDIBLE], and I'm hungry. It's nothing. It's-- it's hunger. It's-- fine, it's very hungry, extremely hungry. But not to eat for weeks, it's-- it's something you can't-- even if you would go today on hunger for the time I was hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because your body has still got something in reserve. But when you have nothing and be hungry, it's something you can't-- can't explain. And with the hunger, other things, you know? However much you think, I don't care if I die tomorrow. But you do care.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] you're so busy being hungry that you don't quite think about that point, that part. It's-- it's so big. And I never wanted to talk about it, because I always felt others undermined. Or, if nobody would have spoke, just one or two, people you have said it's exaggerated. Now, that everybody's speaking about it, it doesn't sound so exaggerated. But I would have talked about it 30 years ago, oh, yeah, oh, yeah. You know, nobody would have kind of quite grasped the whole thing. But if everybody talks about it, somehow somebody will believe it. It's-- I found-- I found it always too-- not degrading. [INAUDIBLE] hard to talk about it. It didn't mean anything. And anybody would have believed it. Like, my children would have believed it. I didn't want to hurt them. I didn't want them to make me into this martyr. But now I'm not the only one. You know, it's a lot of people doing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm not a martyr. I'm-- I'm very proud of what I've achieved. And I'm not a martyr. And other people probably suffered more. So it's very hard to say why didn't I talk about it before. There are a million reasons. Well, I want to thank you very much for talking about it now.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope it will be to some use for somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you'd like to add before we turn off the tape and look at the pictures and meet your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. There is so much to add and so much to think about that it's-- I don't know which part is more important or what would-- I don't know. No, I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think you have got the gist of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome. This picture is my grandfather. I don't know when it was taken. I found it at the cousin of mine. His name was [INAUDIBLE] Birnbaum. And he was the first settler from Poland into Hungary at the time in the 18th century. No further dates, but a cousin of mine sent me this picture. And this is the only one which I found. I didn't have any pictures. This picture are-- is my entire family. On the right is my father Zigmund Birnbaum and [INAUDIBLE] Birnbaum. Next to it is my mother, Margaret Birnbaum. On the right is my older brother, [INAUDIBLE] Birnbaum. In the middle, it's me, Eva Birnbaum Neumann. And the left-hand side is my youngest brother. His name was Isaac Birnbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the-- you were the oldest child, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the oldest one, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so the one to your right--

speaker: interviewee
text: To my right is a year--

speaker: interviewer
text: --is the older of the two brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, three younger brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. And this was taken in our town. I don't know what year. And this picture was also found by some relations abroad who sent me a few years ago. And this is a copy of it. This picture was also found by some relation. In the pram is my little brother called [INAUDIBLE] Birnbaum. And next to him, it's me, Eva Birnbaum Neumann. And this was taken in front of our house when I was two years old. That's the only picture I've got left of myself and my brother. This picture was taken approximately 1934 in our back garden. In the middle, it's me Eva Birnbaum Neumann. On my right is my older brother, [INAUDIBLE] Birnbaum. And on my left is Isaac Birnbaum, my baby brother. That's the only one's I've got left. This picture was taken 18 months ago at my youngest daughter's wedding. On the left is my son [INAUDIBLE] Neumann. Next to him is his wife Annette. Then it's my daughter Devorah Goldberg, her husband [INAUDIBLE] Goldberg. Next to him is the groom, who is Ariel Bergash. The bride is Leah, Leah Neumann. Next to it, it's me Eva Neumann. Then it's my older daughter Miriam Goldberg. Next to her is her husband David Goldberg. Then it's my older son Yehoshua Neumann. And next to him is his wife, Sylvia Neumann. This is a picture of my youngest daughter, my husband, and me. That's Leah Neumann on the left and my husband Leopold Neumann and me, Eva Neumann at one of my grandson's bar mitzvahs. His name was [? Uri ?] Goldberg. This picture was taken in Israel at my youngest daughter's Leah Bergash's wedding. These were the bridesmaids. [INAUDIBLE] on the wedding 18 months ago. They're all cousins, brothers sisters, and cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are these your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are all my grandchildren. Not all, some of my grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everyone in this picture is your grandchild.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every one in this--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you have more than grandchildren that--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have got more grandchildren than this. But I haven't got a picture here with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please introduce your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Ariel, my son-in-law, just got married to Leah about two years ago. And this is their little baby. Her name is Deborah Bergash.

speaker: interviewer
text: We've just been speaking to your mother for a while about-- about her history and about her experience in the war. Is this a story that you've heard as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard parts of it throughout my life more recently rather than when I was younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is it like to hear? What does it mean to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It hurts. It hurts a lot to think she went through that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've just read it in the course of the last year. Because we were living in Manchester. And so I had an opportunity to speak to my mother about it. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the one thing that struck me was what her father told her, that she has to survive and just always think to that end. I mean, it just really touched me. And-- and I'm grateful to [INAUDIBLE] that she did. Because she's a great mother-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, Ariel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Deborah's named after-- after--

speaker: interviewee
text: Her mom.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-in-law's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you all. Is there anything else that you would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're very-- we're very happy she did it. It's good. It's good for us. Hopefully, it'll be good for her and for everybody that's listening to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Say-- say, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
